english: ṗu-rón (phu ron) Pth., ṗug-rón (phug ron), (*ṗur-gón* (*phur gon*) vulg.) pigeon; ṗu-rón-gyi kyu (phu ron gyi kyu) Pth.; ṗug-skyá (phug skya) Sch. of a light blue colour, like pigeons.
Ld. (from the Turkish), pilaw, a dish of boiled rice, with butter and dried apricots.
mouse, souslik and similar rodent quadrupeds (cf. bra (bra)).

1. = ṗugs (phugs). -- 2. = sbúgs (sbugs), ṗúg-pa (phug pa); lgáṅ-ṗug-gaṅ (lgang phug gang), the bladder, in reference to its capaciousness, S.g.; mǰe ṗúg-tu nub (mje phug tu nub), the penis recedes into its cavity, Wdn.; the eye of a needle, Lt. -- 3. pf. and imp. of obug-pa ('bug pa). -- 4 = ṗub (phub) Schr. -- 5. for ṗug-rón (phug ron), q.v.

cavern; brag-ṗúg (brag phug), rock-cavern, grotto; gad-ṗúg (gad phug), cavern in a steep river-bank, or in conglomerate; dbéṅ-ṗug (dben phug), the solitary cavern of an anchorite, Ma.; ṗúg-pa-pa (phug pa pa), n. of an astronomer of the 15th. century, v. pád-ma (pad ma); ṗug-rtsís (phug rtsis), and likewise ṗug-lúgs (phug lugs) Wdn., his calculations.
(?) W., hoopoe; perh. = ṗu-śúd (phu shud), which occasionally is also spelled ṗu-γśud (phu gshud).

(cognate to ṗu (phu); also obúg-pa ('bug pa) and sbúgs (sbugs)), end, termination; ṗug-mda-ṫúg-pai lóṅ-ka (phug mda' thug pa'i long ka), the entrails, the beginning and end of which lie close together, Mil. (mda (mda'), v. under ṗu (phu)); innermost part, an innermost apartment, = sbúgs (sbugs); ṗúgs-kyi nor (phugs kyi nor) v. sgo (sgo) init.; perh. also ṗúg-gi spa-rím (phug gi spa rim) ltá-bu (lta bu) Glr. 45, 4 may be referable to this meaning. séms-kyi ṗugs-ṫag ċod-pas bde (sems kyi phugs thag chod pas bde), happy (am I). because the final aim of my mind is decided and settled, Mil., evidently = ṗu-ṫag ċod-pa (phu thag chod pa), the former being perh. etymologically more correct. Similarly: bu ṫse odii bló-ṗugs ċós-la γtód-čig (bu tshe 'di'i blo phugs chos la gtod cig) Mil., may the boy direct the aim of his mind for this life unto religion! -- Time to come, futurity, (opp. to ṗral (phral), the present moment); ṗúgs-su (phugs su), ṗugs-na (phugs na), hereafter, at last, ultimately (Sch. always?); ṗugs-či odra čig oṅ (phugs ci 'dra cig 'ong), how will it end? what will be the final issue? Glr.
ṗógs-ta (phogs ta), pogs-ta (pogs ta), W. (Pers. پخته), firm, strong, durable; ṗúgs-ta btsems (phugs ta btsems), sew it well (so that it will hold)!

1. heap; ṗuṅ-por spuṅ-ba (phung por spung ba), Lex. also bčér-ba (bcer ba), to gather into a heap; nás-ṗuṅ (nas phung), rtsá-ṗuṅ (rtsa phung), lúd-ṗuṅ (lud phung), sá-ṗuṅ (sa phung), a heap of barley, hay, dung, earth; mass, me-múr-gyi ṗúṅ-po (me mur gyi phung po), a glowing mass, a mass of fire; sprín-ṗuṅ (sprin phung), clouds, a gathering of clouds Glr.; γnyér-mai ṗúṅ-po (gnyer ma'i phung po) (the skin becomes) a heap of wrinkles, Thgy.; the body is called mi-γtsáṅ-ba rnám-pa sna-ṫsógs-kyi ṗuṅ-po (mi gtsang ba rnam pa sna tshogs kyi phung po), dug γsúm-gyi ṗuṅ-po (dug gsum gyi phung po), oǰig-pai ṗuṅ-po ('jig pa'i phung po), zín-pai ṗuṅ-po (zin pa'i phung po), Thgy.; accumulation, mass, bsod-nams-kyi (bsod nams kyi), ċós-kyi (chos kyi), e.g. ċós-kyi ṗuṅ-po 84 000 (chos kyi phung po 84 000), the whole mass of the 84 000 religious lectures of Buddha (!) Mil. -- 2. In metaphysics: स्कन्ध, the so-called five aggregates (Cs.) or elements of being, viz. γzugs (gzugs), ṫsór-ba (tshor ba), odu-śes ('du shes), odu-byéd ('du byed), rnam-śés (rnam shes), (v. Köpp. I. 602, and esp. Burn. I. 475 and 511), which in the physical process of conception unite, so as to form a human individual or the body of a man, (ṗuṅ-po lṅa-las grub-pai lus (phung po lnga las grub pa'i lus) Wdn.) which by some of the later and more popular writers is itself called ṗuṅ-po (phung po). So this word, as being synonymous to lus (lus), has found its way into the language of the people, and not in a low sense, in as much as one of our Christian converts used the expression: ye-šui ṗuṅ-po dur-k̇uṅ-ne żeṅs (ye shu'i phung po dur khung ne zhengs). -- 3. Symb. num. for 5.

sbst. 1. (v. oṗud-pa ('phud pa), pf. ṗud (phud)), a thing set apart, used particularly of the first-fruits of the field, as a meat- or drink-offering, in various applications: zas-ċáṅ-gi ṗud (zas chang gi phud) meat- and drink-offering Glr.; tóg-ṗud (tog phud), ló-ṗud (lo phud), an offering of the first-fruits of harvest; srús-ṗud (srus phud) id., consisting of ears of corn, wound round a pillar of
